title: How does the rank of an elliptic curve grow in towers of number fields?
speaker: Florian Sprung
abstract: On an elliptic curve $y^2=x^3+ax+b$, the points with coordinates $(x,y)$ in a given number field form a finitely generated abelian group. One natural question is how the rank of this group changes when changing the number field. For the simplest example with infinitely many number fields, fix a prime $p$. Adjoining to $\mathbf{Q}$ the $p$th, $p^2$th, $p^3$th,... roots of unity produces a tower of number fields \[\mathbf{Q} \subset \mathbf{Q}(\zeta_p)\subset \mathbf{Q}(\zeta_{p^2})\subset ....

proof_stages.label: Cyclotomic Z_p tower over Q and bounded rank
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Mordell-Weil finite generation and setting the rank notation r(F), the speaker constructs the cyclotomic Z_p-extension Q_infinity/Q and states the Kato-Rohrlich boundedness phenomenon for ranks in its finite layers.

proof_stages.label: Imaginary quadratic Z_p^2 tower and Mazur's boundedness problem
proof_stages.summary: For an imaginary quadratic field K, the speaker introduces the full Z_p^2-extension, its cyclotomic and anticyclotomic Z_p subextensions, recalls known anticyclotomic growth phenomena, and frames Mazur's conjecture that all other Z_p-extensions should have bounded rank.

proof_stages.label: Main O(p^n) rank-growth theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: if p splits in K and p is prime to the class number of K, then the rank over the nth layer of any Z_p-extension, more generally over the nth layer K_n of the Z_p^2-extension, grows at most on the order of p^n.

proof_stages.label: Ordinary Selmer-control argument
proof_stages.summary: In the ordinary case p does not divide a_p, ranks are bounded by studying p-primary Selmer groups M_n and their direct limit as modules over the Iwasawa algebra, using Mazur control plus Kato/Rohrlich or Van Order torsion theorems to estimate coinvariants.

proof_stages.label: Plus/minus control when a_p=0
proof_stages.summary: When p divides a_p with a_p=0, the speaker invokes Kobayashi-Pollack-Kim plus/minus Selmer theory, whose separate plus and minus Selmer groups satisfy analogues of the torsion and control results and therefore give the same kind of rank bound.

proof_stages.label: Failure of ordinary control and fine-Selmer quotient in the hard supersingular case
proof_stages.summary: For the remaining case p divides a_p but a_p is nonzero, ordinary and plus/minus control fail because local points shrink in the tower, so the proof isolates what can still be controlled via localization maps and a fine Selmer quotient.

proof_stages.label: Cuoco-Monsky decomposition and rare determinant vanishing
proof_stages.summary: The remaining kernel is bounded using the Cuoco-Monsky isotypical decomposition for two-variable Iwasawa modules, reducing growth to local isotypical components whose nonzero contribution is controlled by the rare vanishing of a 2-by-2 localization determinant, yielding the final O(p^n) estimate.

examples.name: finite-abelian-group analogy for torsion Iwasawa modules
examples.note: The speaker asks the audience to imagine replacing the Iwasawa algebra by Z, so that a torsion module is just a finite abelian group, illustrating the meaning of Kato's torsion theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only uses finite abelian groups as an analogy for finitely generated torsion Z_p[[X]]-modules, without computing or checking anything on a specific finite abelian group.

passes.reason: A finite abelian group is a standard torsion object used only as an analogy for one-variable torsion Iwasawa modules.
finite abelian groups are used only as a standard analogy for torsion Iwasawa modules, not to refute a claim

examples.name: one-relation two-variable Iwasawa module toy model
examples.note: A toy torsion module over a two-variable power-series/Iwasawa algebra is used to make concrete why coinvariants over the nth layer can have Z_p-rank of order p^n.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker introduces a toy two-variable torsion module and reasons through its fixed/dual part, concluding that the resulting Z_p-rank is p^n.

passes.reason: The toy cyclic quotient of a two-variable Iwasawa algebra collapses one variable and is used to show that two-variable torsion need not give finite Z_p-rank.
the cyclic two-variable module collapses one Iwasawa direction and illustrates that two-variable torsion need not give finite Z_p-rank
The toy module is essentially a two-variable Iwasawa algebra quotient with one variable/relation collapsing it to a simpler torsion model, used illustratively rather than adversarially.

The cyclic two-variable quotient, essentially [0m[0mLambda/(X) or [0m[0mLambda/(Y), collapses one variable yet realizes the maximal [0m[0mp^n finite-level rank growth, showing the one-variable bounded-rank intuition fails.
The cyclic one-relation two-variable module is a standard torsion toy model used to show that the one-variable bounded-rank intuition fails in two variables.
The cyclic one-relation module over the two-variable Iwasawa algebra is a standard toy torsion module used to show the naive one-variable bounded-rank behavior fails in two variables.
The toy module such as p[[X,Y]]/(X) collapses one variable and gives the tight p^n-rank growth, showing two-variable torsion need not imply finite p-rank.
the toy module, essentially Z_p[[X,Y]]/(X), collapses one variable into a codimension-one boundary case and is used to show two-variable torsion need not give bounded Z_p-ranks.
A cyclic two-variable Iwasawa module such as a coordinate quotient collapses one variable and gives p^n coinvariant growth, showing the bounded one-variable argument cannot generalize.
The cyclic one-relation two-variable module is used to show the one-variable finite-rank torsion statement fails in two variables, while structurally it is a standard height-one torsion module.

The toy module, essentially a two-variable Iwasawa algebra quotient killing one variable, illustrates the failure of the one-variable bounded-rank intuition by producing coinvariants of rank about p^n.
A toy module such as Z_p[[X,Y]]/(X) shows the one-variable torsion-implies-finite-Z_p-rank intuition fails in two variables, and structurally collapses one variable of the two-variable algebra.
